news_summary: Recent advancements have validated a long-standing vitamin B1 theory and initiated a UN AI panel focused on human-centered global impact studies.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes ont confirmé une théorie de longue date concernant la vitamine B1 et ont donné lieu à la création d'un groupe d'experts de l'ONU sur l'intelligence artificielle, chargé de mener des études d'impact mondial centrées sur l'humain.
news_summary_es: Los avances recientes han confirmado una teoría sobre la vitamina B1 que se venía barajando desde hace tiempo y han dado lugar a la creación de un grupo de expertos de la ONU en inteligencia artificial dedicado a estudios de impacto global centrados en las personas.

articles.title: A 67-year-old “crazy” theory about vitamin B1 has finally been proven
articles.summary: Scientists have achieved the unthinkable by stabilizing a highly reactive molecule in water, confirming a decades-old theory about vitamin B1’s role in the body. The breakthrough not only solves a scientific mystery but could revolutionize greener chemical manufacturing.
articles.content:
Chemists have achieved what many once considered impossible by stabilizing an extremely reactive molecule in water, confirming a 67-year-old theory about vitamin B1. The breakthrough not only resolves a long-standing biochemical puzzle, but also points toward cleaner, more efficient methods for producing pharmaceuticals.

At the center of the discovery is a carbene, a form of carbon with just six valence electrons. Under normal conditions, carbon atoms are most stable with eight electrons. With only six, carbenes are highly unstable and react almost instantly with their surroundings. In water, they typically break down right away.

For decades, scientists believed that vitamin B1, also known as thiamine, might briefly form a carbene-like structure inside cells to help drive essential biochemical reactions. However, because of the molecule's extreme instability, no one had been able to directly observe it in such conditions.

First Stable Carbene Observed in Water

Researchers have now succeeded in creating a carbene that remains stable in water. Not only did they generate it, they also isolated it, sealed it in a tube, and observed it staying intact for months. The findings are detailed in a study published in Science Advances.

"This is the first time anyone has been able to observe a stable carbene in water," said Vincent Lavallo, a professor of chemistry at UC Riverside and corresponding author of the paper. "People thought this was a crazy idea. But it turns out, Breslow was right."

A 1958 Hypothesis Finally Confirmed

Lavallo is referring to Ronald Breslow, a Columbia University chemist who proposed in 1958 that vitamin B1 could transform into a carbene to enable key biochemical reactions. While the idea was influential, it remained unproven because carbenes were known to be too unstable, especially in water, to capture or study.

To overcome this challenge, Lavallo's team developed a protective molecular structure that surrounds the carbene. He describes it as "a suit of armor," designed to shield the reactive center from water and other nearby molecules. With this protection, the carbene becomes stable enough for detailed analysis using nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy and x-ray crystallography, offering clear evidence that such molecules can exist in water.

"We were making these reactive molecules to explore their chemistry, not chasing a historical theory," said first author Varun Raviprolu, who completed the research as a graduate student at UCR and is now a postdoctoral researcher at UCLA. "But it turns out our work ended up confirming exactly what Breslow proposed all those years ago."

Toward Greener Chemistry and Drug Production

The implications go beyond solving a scientific mystery. Carbenes are widely used as "ligands," or supporting components in metal-based catalysts that help drive chemical reactions. These catalysts play a major role in producing pharmaceuticals, fuels, and other materials. However, many of these processes depend on toxic organic solvents.

By stabilizing carbenes in water, the researchers may have opened the door to safer and more environmentally friendly chemical production.

"Water is the ideal solvent -- it's abundant, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly," Raviprolu said. "If we can get these powerful catalysts to work in water, that's a big step toward greener chemistry."

Closer to Mimicking Chemistry in Living Cells

The ability to create and maintain reactive intermediate molecules in water also brings scientists closer to replicating the chemistry that naturally occurs inside living cells, which are mostly composed of water.

"There are other reactive intermediates we've never been able to isolate, just like this one," Lavallo said. "Using protective strategies like ours, we may finally be able to see them, and learn from them."

A Milestone Years in the Making

For Lavallo, who has spent two decades working with carbenes, the achievement carries both scientific and personal significance.

"Just 30 years ago, people thought these molecules couldn't even be made," he said. "Now we can bottle them in water. What Breslow said all those years ago -- he was right."

Raviprolu sees the breakthrough as a broader lesson about persistence in science.

"Something that seems impossible today might be possible tomorrow, if we continue to invest in science," he said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in chemistry by stabilizing a reactive molecule in water, confirming a decades-old theory about vitamin B1. This achievement has broad implications for greener chemical manufacturing and pharmaceutical production, offering tangible environmental and industrial benefits. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful advancement with potential large-scale positive impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists confirm 67-year-old vitamin B1 theory by stabilizing rare molecule in water
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For nearly 70 years, scientists have wondered if vitamin B1 (also called thiamine) could form a special, highly reactive molecule called a carbene inside our bodies. Carbenes are usually too unstable to last, especially in water, so no one had ever seen one living inside cells.

**What happened** – A team led by chemistry professor Vincent Lavallo at UC Riverside managed to create a carbene that stays stable in water. They surrounded it with a protective “armor” that stops it from breaking down. This allowed them to observe the carbene for months and prove that the old theory from 1958 was correct.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it solves a long-standing mystery about how vitamin B1 works in our cells. It also shows a new way to make chemical reactions happen safely in water, instead of using toxic chemicals. This could lead to cleaner and greener methods for making medicines and other products.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use this protective method to study other unstable molecules inside water. This will help them better understand how chemical reactions happen in living cells and improve environmentally friendly manufacturing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, researchers have stabilized a reactive carbene molecule in water, confirming a decades-old vitamin B1 theory and opening doors to greener chemistry.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques confirment une théorie vieille de 67 ans sur la vitamine B1 en stabilisant une molécule rare dans l'eau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis près de 70 ans, les scientifiques se demandent si la vitamine B1 (ou thiamine) peut former une molécule très réactive appelée carbène dans notre corps. Les carbènes sont généralement trop instables pour durer, surtout dans l'eau, donc personne n’en avait jamais vu dans les cellules.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par le professeur de chimie Vincent Lavallo de l'UC Riverside a réussi à créer un carbène stable dans l'eau. Ils l'ont entouré d'une « armure » protectrice qui l'empêche de se décomposer. Cela leur a permis d'observer le carbène pendant des mois et de prouver que la vieille théorie de 1958 était correcte.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle résout un mystère ancien sur le fonctionnement de la vitamine B1 dans nos cellules. Elle montre aussi une nouvelle façon de faire des réactions chimiques en toute sécurité dans l'eau, au lieu d’utiliser des produits toxiques. Cela pourrait mener à des méthodes plus propres et écologiques pour fabriquer des médicaments et d'autres produits.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser cette méthode protectrice pour étudier d’autres molécules instables dans l'eau. Cela les aidera à mieux comprendre les réactions chimiques dans les cellules vivantes et à améliorer la fabrication respectueuse de l’environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des chercheurs ont stabilisé une molécule carbène réactive dans l'eau, confirmant une théorie vieille de plusieurs décennies sur la vitamine B1 et ouvrant la voie à une chimie plus écologique.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos confirman teoría de 67 años sobre la vitamina B1 al estabilizar una molécula rara en agua
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante casi 70 años, los científicos se preguntaron si la vitamina B1 (también llamada tiamina) podía formar una molécula muy reactiva llamada carbino dentro de nuestro cuerpo. Los carbenos suelen ser demasiado inestables para durar, especialmente en agua, por lo que nadie había visto uno dentro de las células.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo dirigido por el profesor de química Vincent Lavallo en UC Riverside logró crear un carbino que se mantiene estable en agua. Lo rodearon con una “armadura” protectora que evita que se descomponga. Esto les permitió observar el carbino durante meses y probar que la antigua teoría de 1958 era correcta.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque resuelve un misterio de larga data sobre cómo funciona la vitamina B1 en nuestras células. También muestra una nueva forma de realizar reacciones químicas de manera segura en agua, en lugar de usar sustancias tóxicas. Esto podría llevar a métodos más limpios y ecológicos para fabricar medicinas y otros productos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar este método protector para estudiar otras moléculas inestables en agua. Esto les ayudará a entender mejor cómo ocurren las reacciones químicas en las células vivas y a mejorar la fabricación respetuosa con el medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Por primera vez, los investigadores estabilizaron una molécula carbino reactiva en agua, confirmando una teoría de vitamina B1 de décadas y abriendo puertas a una química más verde.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing molecular “armor” gently encasing a delicate, radiant carbon atom suspended in a clear water droplet, symbolizing protection and stability, set against a soft, natural background of flowing water and subtle chemical structures rendered as elegant, abstract shapes.

articles.title: Putting humans at the centre: UN AI panel begins work on global impact study
articles.summary: The UN’s Independent International Scientific Panel on AI – the first global body of its kind – is gearing up for its inaugural in-person summit.
articles.content:
Tasked with navigating the volatile intersection of innovation and ethics, this group of world-leading experts is launching a landmark study into the forces transforming modern life.

“We are not just focusing on AI as a mathematical or algorithmic field: we are also looking at ensuring that humans are central to decision-making,” says Menna El-Assady, an assistant professor at the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology (ETH) in Zurich.

Ms. El-Assady is one of the founding members of the UN’s independent panel on AI, recommended to serve by the UN Secretary-General.Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, the first global scientific body on Artificial Intelligence (AI), which brings together leading experts to assess how AI is transforming our lives.

05-04-2026-UNNews-AIPanel-Assadi-Final.jpg

An Egyptian national, she is one of 40 members, who were formally appointed by the General Assembly in February. They are from various different backgrounds – including academia, the private sector, civil society, government/international organisations, and the technical community – and have backgrounds in core technical AI expertise; applied AI, safety and infrastructure experience; and AI policy, ethics and impact.

Augmented Intelligence

“A human in the machine” is a phrase that is often used in relation to the use of AI. It refers to the idea that a human should always be involved in decisions made by AI tools.

“We are trying to work out when we need to rely on humans and their expertise, and when things can be automated,” she says. “We need to understand the link between AI and human models, what's known as the co-adaptation loop, and the evolution that occurs whenever humans receive new information, or when AI does.

At ETH Zurich, Ms. El-Assady has developed the concept of “augmented intelligence,” using AI to enhance human capabilities rather than replacing humans altogether, and building cooperation between AI and people in various fields.

This expertise is particularly useful for the work of the AI Panel, which is examining the ways that the use of AI is affecting different areas of society, such as the labour market and the health system.

Ms El-Assady is advocating for a “public digital infrastructure,” so that everyone who wants to develop AI has the resources they need.

“We also need to look at how to incorporate different cultures and languages within AI models so that they are not limited to a small number of countries,” she adds.

Trust, ethics and watermarks

The launch of the panel was a reflection of the growing concerns about the risks of unregulated AI, not least within the UN.

UN Secretary-General António Guterres warned the Security Council in September 2025 that “humanity’s fate cannot be left to an algorithm,” whilst Volker Türk, the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights, said in February that AI developers building models without an understanding of fundamental social and ethical principles risk creating “Frankenstein’s monster.”

Ms. El-Assady agrees that ethics and trust are crucial to the sector, as is an awareness of the limitations of AI models.

One possible solution put forward by Ms. El-Assady is “AI watermarking”, which would make it clear whether or not content has been human-originated or AI-generated, and how to distinguish between them.

These are examples of the kinds of topics that could be included in the Scientific Panel’s first report, which is due to be released at the Global Dialogue on AI Governance, which takes place on 6–7 July in Geneva.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes the formation and work of the UN's first global scientific panel on AI, which aims to study AI's societal impact with a focus on ethics, human-centered decision-making, and inclusivity. This initiative has broad significance for global AI governance, public trust, and ethical technology development, offering a substantial positive impact on society.
articles.personality_title: UN launches first global AI panel to study human impact and ethics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations has created its first global scientific panel focused on artificial intelligence (AI). This group brings together experts from many fields to study how AI is changing society and to make sure people stay at the center of decisions about AI.

**What happened** – In February 2026, the UN General Assembly appointed 40 members to the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI. These experts come from universities, governments, businesses, and civil society. They are beginning their work with a summit and plan to release a report in July 2026. One founding member, Menna El-Assady from ETH Zurich, explained that the panel will study how AI can support humans rather than replace them.

**Impact** – This panel is unique because it looks at AI not just as technology but as something that must respect ethics and human control. For example, the panel will explore ideas like "augmented intelligence," where AI helps people make better decisions. They also want AI to serve diverse cultures and languages, ensuring fairness worldwide. This work aims to build trust in AI and avoid risks from unchecked development.

**What's next step** – The panel will continue studying important topics like how AI affects jobs, health care, and ethics. They will suggest ways to keep humans involved in AI decisions and may propose tools like "AI watermarking" to show if content is made by AI or people. Their first report will be shared at a global meeting in Geneva in July 2026.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new global AI panel is working to ensure AI develops safely and fairly, keeping humans at the heart of technology decisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance le premier panel mondial sur l’IA pour étudier l’impact humain et l’éthique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Nations Unies ont créé leur premier panel scientifique mondial dédié à l’intelligence artificielle (IA). Ce groupe réunit des experts de plusieurs domaines pour étudier comment l’IA transforme la société et pour garantir que les humains restent au centre des décisions concernant l’IA.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En février 2026, l’Assemblée générale de l’ONU a nommé 40 membres pour le Panel scientifique international indépendant sur l’IA. Ces experts viennent des universités, des gouvernements, des entreprises et de la société civile. Ils ont commencé leur travail avec un sommet et prévoient de publier un rapport en juillet 2026. Menna El-Assady, membre fondatrice de l’ETH Zurich, a expliqué que le panel étudiera comment l’IA peut soutenir les humains plutôt que de les remplacer.

**Impact** – Ce panel est unique car il considère l’IA non seulement comme une technologie mais aussi comme un domaine qui doit respecter l’éthique et le contrôle humain. Par exemple, le panel explorera des idées comme « l’intelligence augmentée », où l’IA aide les humains à prendre de meilleures décisions. Ils veulent aussi que l’IA serve différentes cultures et langues, garantissant ainsi l’équité dans le monde entier. Ce travail vise à renforcer la confiance dans l’IA et à éviter les risques liés à un développement non contrôlé.

**Prochaine étape** – Le panel continuera d’étudier des sujets importants comme l’impact de l’IA sur l’emploi, la santé et l’éthique. Ils proposeront des moyens pour que les humains restent impliqués dans les décisions liées à l’IA et pourraient suggérer des outils comme le « filigrane IA » pour indiquer si un contenu est créé par une IA ou par des humains. Leur premier rapport sera présenté lors d’une réunion mondiale à Genève en juillet 2026.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau panel mondial de l’ONU sur l’IA travaille à un développement sûr et équitable de l’IA, en plaçant les humains au cœur des décisions technologiques.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU lanza primer panel global de IA para estudiar impacto humano y ética
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las Naciones Unidas han creado su primer panel científico global dedicado a la inteligencia artificial (IA). Este grupo reúne a expertos de diferentes áreas para estudiar cómo la IA está cambiando la sociedad y asegurar que las personas sigan siendo el centro de las decisiones sobre IA.

**Qué pasó** – En febrero de 2026, la Asamblea General de la ONU nombró a 40 miembros para el Panel Científico Internacional Independiente sobre IA. Estos expertos provienen de universidades, gobiernos, empresas y sociedad civil. Comenzaron su trabajo con una cumbre y planean publicar un informe en julio de 2026. Menna El-Assady, miembro fundadora de ETH Zurich, explicó que el panel estudiará cómo la IA puede apoyar a los humanos en lugar de reemplazarlos.

**Impacto** – Este panel es único porque no ve la IA solo como una tecnología, sino como algo que debe respetar la ética y el control humano. Por ejemplo, explorarán la "inteligencia aumentada", donde la IA ayuda a las personas a tomar mejores decisiones. También quieren que la IA sirva a diferentes culturas e idiomas, garantizando justicia en todo el mundo. Este trabajo busca generar confianza en la IA y evitar riesgos por desarrollos sin control.

**Próximo paso** – El panel seguirá estudiando temas importantes como el impacto de la IA en el empleo, la salud y la ética. Propondrán formas para que los humanos sigan involucrados en las decisiones de IA y podrían sugerir herramientas como la "marca de agua de IA" para mostrar si un contenido fue creado por IA o personas. Su primer informe se presentará en una reunión global en Ginebra en julio de 2026.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El nuevo panel global de IA de la ONU trabaja para que la IA se desarrolle de forma segura y justa, manteniendo a las personas en el centro de las decisiones tecnológicas.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious circle of diverse, glowing human silhouettes connected by intertwining, translucent neural-like threads to a central, softly illuminated abstract globe symbolizing global AI, set against a warm, earthy background with subtle layers representing digital networks and cultural diversity.
